title_en: The Arabian Peninsula — Religious and Social Conditions
title_ar: الأوضاع الدينية والاجتماعية في الجزيرة العربية
content_en:
Before examining the life of the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, al-Mubarakpuri devotes considerable attention to the religious, social, and moral landscape of pre-Islamic Arabia — the period known in Islamic tradition as al-Jahiliyyah, the Age of Ignorance. Understanding this context is essential for appreciating both the profound transformation that Islam brought and the magnitude of the Prophet's ﷺ achievement in reforming an entire civilization within a single generation.

Religiously, pre-Islamic Arabia was characterized by a polytheism layered over a much older Abrahamic heritage. The Quran records that the Arabs themselves acknowledged Allah as the Creator and Sustainer — 'If you ask them who created the heavens and the earth, they will certainly say: Allah' (Quran 29:61) — but they associated various deities and idols with Him as intermediaries and intercessors. The Kaaba, originally built by Ibrahim (Abraham) and his son Ismail for the pure worship of Allah, had by the time of the Prophet ﷺ become surrounded by 360 idols, representing the tribal gods of various Arabian communities. The most prominent included Hubal, the chief idol of the Quraysh; al-Lat, al-Uzza, and Manat — the three daughters falsely attributed to Allah by the Arabs.

Beyond the dominant polytheism, pockets of other religious traditions existed across Arabia. Communities of Jews had settled in several locations, particularly in Medina (then called Yathrib) and in Khaybar. Christian communities existed in Najran in the south and among the Ghassanid Arabs of the north. Hanifs — monotheists who had rejected idol worship and sought to follow the original religion of Ibrahim without accepting Judaism or Christianity — were scattered across the Peninsula. Among the most famous of these was Waraqah ibn Nawfal, the cousin of Khadijah bint Khuwaylid, who would later be among the first to recognize Muhammad's ﷺ prophetic call.

Socially, pre-Islamic Arab society was organized primarily along tribal lines. The tribe (qabilah) was the fundamental unit of political, economic, and moral organization. Tribal loyalty (asabiyyah) was the supreme social bond, frequently overriding justice and individual conscience. Intertribal warfare was chronic and often sustained by cycles of revenge that could continue for generations — the famous wars of al-Basus and Dahis wa'l-Ghabra' lasted for decades over trivial offenses. The strong protected the weak within the tribe but had no obligation toward those outside it.

Gender relations in Jahili Arabia present a mixed picture. Arab women of the Jahiliyyah period were sometimes strong, independently-minded figures — Khadijah herself was a successful merchant who employed men and chose her own husband. But female infanticide (wa'd al-banat — burying infant girls alive) was a documented practice, born of a combination of poverty and shame, and the Quran condemns it explicitly: 'And when the female infant who was buried alive is asked for what sin was she killed' (Quran 81:8-9). Marriage practices were varied and sometimes exploitative. Economic inequality was stark — the wealthy merchants of Quraysh accumulated vast fortunes while the poor, slaves, and orphans had minimal protection.

This was the world into which Muhammad ﷺ was born and to which he was sent as a mercy: 'And We have not sent you except as a mercy to all the worlds' (Quran 21:107). The depth of the transformation he achieved — the elimination of idol worship, the establishment of moral law, the liberation of slaves, the protection of women, and the unification of fractious tribes into a single brotherhood — is fully comprehensible only against this backdrop of what pre-Islamic Arabia actually looked like.
